figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Existing recommendation agents personalize what they know (memory) but reason about every user with a fixed, shared policy. (b) SAGER equips each user with a self-evolving policy skill that personalizes how the agent reasons, not just what it remembers.

Figure 4: i2Agent with and without SAGER skill evolution. Skill evolution provides additive gains on top of dynamic profile updates, confirming orthogonality between profile-level memory and policy-level skill.
Figure 5: Vanilla LLM with and without SAGER skill evolution. Skill evolution yields consistent improvements across all datasets, demonstrating the module’s practical utility.
Table 3: Main results for Yelp and MovieTV. Notation follows Table 2; all improvements are significant (p < 0.05).
Table 2: Main results for Goodreads and Books. All improvements are statistically significant (p < 0.05). Underline=best baseline, Bold=best overall.

Figure 2: The SAGER framework operates in four stages per interaction: 1⃝ Retrieve: the Memory Manager (LLMMem) curates top-k neighbors from the semantic graph and synthesizes collaborative facets Mcollab; 2⃝ Extract: the full policy skill repository S(t−1)

Figure 6: Effect of warmup rounds τ on H@1, H@3, and H@5 (one panel per metric; bar colors distinguish datasets). τ = 2 achieves the best performance across most metrics and datasets (yellow highlight).

Figure 7: Effect of slim skill injection length on H@1, H@3, and H@5 (one panel per metric; line colors distinguish datasets). Yellow region: Cognitive Injection Boundary (30 tokens); red region: over-injection zone (≥150 tokens).
Figure 3: iAgent with and without SAGER skill evolution across four datasets. Consistent improvements across all datasets demonstrate that skill evolution is effective even for static-profile agents.
